sentences.text: No, I had no idea.
Right.
Exactly.
That lasted a few months.
I think she recognized that I was able to be able to handle it on my own.
And then I pretty well blended in fairly nicely and was able to learn English, stay with my school.
And, at that point, I forgot what grade they put me in, but I think I remember they had to put me in a couple of earlier grades just to get started.
And then when I was probably 13, 12, 13, I was already jumped in, and I was already caught up in my regular grades.
So, the native intelligence was there.
It was just a matter of me translating it into the English way of life, the American way of life.
So, then I just moved OK into that.
So, I wasn't held back.
I wasn't sitting in a kindergarten class when I was older.
And then I learned English fairly quickly.
Picked it up.
I spoke very little German.
I refused.
I had a lot of problems.
The war, I think the experiences, it affected my parents, my stepfather, my mother tremendously.
The experience that they went through-- I think my stepfather went through some very bad in Buchenwald and Bergen-Belsen.
And he saw a lot.
He never talked about it at all.
I never knew anything while I was living with them.
They never discussed anything, even though I know they went through it.
I know that my mother went through a lot.
It was just never a discussion.
Whenever it came up with other survivors, they would just shut up.
They would never enter-- they would never join in the conversations.
Although some of their friends or people that they knew who were in the camps talked more about it, they never would.
And, so, I grew up always with this idea that you just don't talk about it.
But they held a lot in, too, and it just made life very, very bad for us all around.
And then, actually, I left home.
I mean, I just left home when I was about 14.
Not really.
Not really, because both being German, it was pretty well-known the attitude that the German Jews had.
And I think there was a lot of-- and I don't want to psychoanalyze German Jews, but I think there was a lot of ambiguity going on between German Jews.
On the one hand, they always felt that they were sort of a little bit better and classier than the rest of the European Jews.
But at other hand, they realized that didn't help them any.
They had to go through the same horrors that everybody else went through, and I think it just never reconciled in their own lives.
And the other thing that bothered me.
It bothered me more in later life than it did at that time, but it bothers me more now as I look back is that they kept identifying with Germany all this time.
I know that we have met a lot of other Jews, Polish Jews, Russian Jews, Czechoslovakian, Dutch, all that.
They recognize that they were not wanted by their country.
Their country turned their backs on them.
And they were very happy to be in America.
With the German Jews, or at least the friends that we had, they only associated with German Jews, other German Jews who were in the camps.
A lot of them wanted to go back to Germany.
A lot of them kept visiting Germany.
And it wasn't until years later, I'm saying, what are you guys doing?
You know, they would never be able to make that separation.
And they kept speaking German at home all the time.
They just never wanted to give that up.
I guess those roots were so ingrained in them.
And they just-- they gave me no Judaism.
Although my stepfather wasn't observant, but High Holy Days, he would go to temple.
My mother never went to temple at all.
I never had that.
They attempted to send me to get a Jewish education in the Lower East Side when I was-- when we came over, but it was your archetypal Jewish scholar.
I mean, the one that you see in the movie.
The little walk-up flat.
The beard, and the yarmulke, and the dingy hall.
It just wasn't-- I didn't mind that so much, but I didn't get this-- I didn't get this philosophical installation of being a Jew.
They just said, go there and learn.
So, it was very rote.
That didn't work very well.
So, finally, they dropped it.
I didn't get encouragement at home.
They didn't give me the value of being a Jew.
They just said, go.
Why?
I don't know.
Because maybe the neighbors were doing it, too, and they just felt that I ought to do it.
So, but I knew I lived in a Jewish neighborhood.
And some of it came through.
But then we would start wondering out.

I started associating with other kids in the neighborhood, and we would wander outside the Jewish neighborhood towards the Italian neighborhood and Hell's Kitchen, and we would get into some altercations there, too.
I mean, we were called dirty kikes, even though we were living in the Lower East Side of New York.
I don't care where it went, it happened.
But, it wasn't as traumatic, because we had a safe haven to go back, and we had friends.
We knew that-- we knew there was just-- you know, we knew that it wasn't sanctioned by the authorities like it was in Germany.
The anti-Semitism wasn't?
Right.
Well, 0 wasn't very large then.
I was tall, but I was skinny.
I wasn't overpowering.
I was just tall and gangly.
When I came to-- when I was-- I think when I was 15 or 16 years old, I may have been six foot tall, but I must have weighed 130 pounds.
I mean, I was just a bag of beans.
My nutrition just never-- it just never caught up with me.
And so, I never had any not very much flesh, you know.
So, but I learned to fight in the streets.
I mean, I learned to take care of myself.
Growing up in New York, you had to.

You had the opportunity to do it, and I think I had a little training in Germany, but you really can't defend yourself against five or 10 kids who really had in their mind to-- but once I came to New York, it was a little different.
I got a little more confidence in myself.
So, that was basically my Jewish background, I mean, right after the war, right after the camps.
Well, life was pretty bad for me.
Life was pretty bad for me, and my mother just really-- just didn't like me very much.
I think I just-- she realized that, the light of day, that I wasn't a love child, and I reminded her of my father.

She probably thought-- and we never really talked very much about it, but just various-- just getting a basic understanding was that I probably reminded her of all the bad things that happened in her life.
And then she married a man who probably wasn't too crazy about me either.
He was marrying a woman that had a little baggage.
He probably didn't have much choice.
I probably didn't take to him.
There wasn't a lot of warmth.
I refused to take his name.
Well, yeah, they asked me.
Yeah.
They asked me if I would change my name to his, and I refused.
Don't ask me why.
I don't know why.
Well, I must have been about-- I don't know, 9 or 10.
Maybe 11.
Well, I came over here, was 11.
It must have been about 11 or 12.
But I saw no-- I saw no benefit of changing to his name.
He didn't like me any more than I liked him.
I think he-- My mother was very brutal with me.
Today, my mother would be charged with child abuse.
In those days, it wasn't an issue, a social issue.
But she would beat me, I mean, regularly beat me, and he would just stand in the back and just allow it to happen.
He would either egg her on or not stop it.
And, so, I looked at him as not being a father figure at all or a role model.
To me, he was just my mother's husband, and I saw no wisdom in taking on his name.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So, my life was pretty bad.
So, when I was about 14, 13 or 14, I don't remember the day, I just left.
I just-- I just got on a bus, and I left.
And I've been on my own ever since.
Well, I went to Texas the first time.
Well, I've ran away.
I mean, I've ran away a few times.
Yeah, before.
I've ran away.
You know, I just couldn't take it.
Ran away.
Police would find me.
Bring me back home.
Why would they bring me back home?
I told them.
I said, I'm going to run away again next week.
And I, you know, was working part-time.
I got some money I was able to hide.
And then one day I just my packed my bags, what little bag I had, and I got onto a Greyhound bus, and I went to Fort Worth.
Went to Texas.
Oh, just deliveries.
You know, whatever I would do after school.
Working for some kind of a delivery service on a bicycle in New York City.
Working for a delicatessen, delivering chickens to somebody, or messenger service, or you know, whatever, typical after-school work that teenage boys would be able to get on a bicycle.
I don't know what I was making. $0.25 an hour or whatever.
Yeah, exactly.
I wasn't getting any allowance.
I had to do it.
So, I was able to-- for some reason, I was able to squirrel away some money, I think, maybe $50 or something.
I don't know.
I had have enough money to buy a bus ticket.
I had enough money to buy a bus ticket to Fort Worth, Texas.
Well, I went into the Greyhound bus station, and I laid down whatever money I had, and I said, how far will this take me?
I mean, this is literally, how far will this take me?
I mean, some idiot behind the glass counter never even looked up.
Never said to me, well, you're too young to travel alone.
He says, I don't know.
Probably Fort Worth.
I said, good, give me a ticket.
He slid a ticket.
I got into a bus.
Went to Fort Worth.
I must have been 14 and a half. 15 maybe.
I, you know, was young.
Obviously, too young to travel by myself, but who cared?
I did it.
I was old enough as far as the Greyhound bus guy was concerned.
Got on the bus and left.
And wound up in Fort Worth.
I had no idea what I was going to do there, but the further I got away from that abusive family of mine, the better off I'd be.
I got off the bus, and I started hitchhiking.
I don't know where I was going.
I was lost.
But it turned out that a fellow picked me up who had a ranch.
And I went to work for him.
He had three boys of his own, and I guess he recognized that I was a runaway.
And, so, he must have recognized that anybody who is going to run away from New York must have had a pretty good reason to run away, all that way.
There's no point in shipping me back.
Besides, what were they going to do?
He said, I'll take care of you.
You just work for me, and I'll give you some schooling and all that.
And, at that time, I think I left-- I don't think I went past the eighth grade or ninth grade.
So, I worked for him for about a year on a ranch.
And when you got off the bus in Fort Worth, it must have looked incredibly different than what you left in New York.
Well, actually, pardon me.
Actually, I got off in Dallas.
I was hitchhiking-- I was hitchhiking to Fort Worth.
For some reason, I got off in Dallas.
And Dallas, even in those days, had skyscrapers.
This was like in-- when was this? 1952? '53?
I don't really recall.
But it was a downtown city.
And I needed to get out of there.
I guess maybe I saw a lot of Hopalong Cassidy movies.
I don't know.
I figured maybe I'll go to work on a ranch.
And sure enough, I wound up working on a ranch.
And this fellow picked me up.
And worked for him for about a year, year and a half.
And he sent me to school, a local school there, and I continued school, but I never did graduate high school at all.
I just-- after a while, I just kept wandering.
I guess he realized that-- I don't know.
It is a very amicable parting.
But maybe I just didn't want to stay there.
Maybe I was beginning to-- maybe I just had this need to keep moving a lot, to travel.
I had no home.
You know, I was always alone.
And I just had this need to continue doing that way.
It was just part of my life.
So, for a number of years, I just traveled.
When I say traveled, I wouldn't call it traveling.
I kept moving.
You know, hitchhiking all over the country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rail cars, hitchhiking, buses.
Whatever mode.
I lived in a lot of different states, a lot of different cities.
One time, I wound up back in New York a few years later, and I called up my mother.
I said, I'm here.
I called her from a payphone.
I need a place to stay.
And she said, I'm sorry.
You know, I got no room for you.
I said, I only need, like, a couple of days on your couch, you know?
Sorry, no room.
Well, I mean, look.
I left home.
She didn't know where I was.
She was rid of me.
So, I said, OK.
And that was-- I never saw her again for a lot of years after that.
You know, I guess that was the way it was going to be.
How would you-- what would you do for money on the road?
Oh, I always managed to find a job someplace.
I was, you know, I was-- for better or for worse, she taught me to be self-sufficient.
There was always work to be had, whether it was washing dishes in a restaurant or it was loading on loading trucks, going to work for a farm for a couple of days, picking tomatoes.
Wherever I happened to be, I always managed to find work, whether it was for a day or a week or a month.
But I always had an honest dollar in my pocket.
Always.
And I learned to eat radishes off the fields, pick tomatoes, find an apple.
Sleep wherever I can.
Bus stations.
Train stations.
Park benches.
Yeah, I'd settle for a month, two months, a week, whatever the situation was.
Yeah, I wound up in Florida.
I lived in Florida for a number of years and actually became a lifeguard down in Florida.
Lived near the racetrack, the [INAUDIBLE] racetrack, became a lifeguard.
Then-- then I left.
I lived there for a couple of years in '55 and '56. 1955, '56.
And then I had to move on.
I guess it was a failed romance, or some immature reason to leave.
And then I started hitchhiking again and started traveling cross-country.
And I wound up in the state of Washington.
Wound up working on a strawberry farm.
I had no idea.
No idea.
I never had a home.
I had no roots.
I had no friends.
I had no family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was lonely.
I remember, you know, maybe hitchhiking or going up and down a street looking at homes and seeing people inside homes.
You know, obviously, very lonely.
Why wasn't I not home?
But knowing that I didn't belong there.
So, for a lot of years, I just sort of wandered on my own.
I was just a hobo there.
I mean, they don't use that term anymore, what but I really was a hobo.
Well, I say a lot of years.
It seems like a lot of years in my life, but it probably was two years, three years.
But they're a long time when you-- while you're experiencing them.
Yeah.
Well, actually, I wound up-- I had heard through some correspondence that-- this was in 1958-- that I was of draft age at that point.
Draft age. 1958.
That I was-- the Army was ready to call me.
I wasn't a citizen in that country.
I was always a green card.
One thing good, I always kept my green card that I had to renew every year.
So, whatever city or state I was in, January of every year, I would renew my green card.
I'd go to the post office and renew it.
But I happened to join the Navy in 1958, early 1958.
And I heard that, if I didn't, I was going to get drafted.
I didn't want to go into the Army.
I wanted a choice.
So, I said, well, let me go ahead and join the Navy.
And I was in Centralia-- Tumwater, where they make Olympia beer.
And I joined the Navy.
They shipped me down to San Diego.
Went to my boot camp there.
And put me through my boot camp, and I became a sailor for the US Navy.
A very honorable thing.
I don't know if I did it because I was honorable, but I was happy to be in America.
And I felt that-- I don't know.
I don't know what was going through my mind.
Maybe I felt-- maybe my life was just probably not getting out of the toilet, and I figured maybe the Navy would have something.
Yeah.
Oh, sure.
As long as I was a legal resident.
In fact, they encouraged you to go in.
And, in fact-- and I don't know if the law is still now or then.
But, in the service, all you have to do is go-- if you're in the service for three years, you are eligible for citizen, as opposed to being here for five years.
So, they gave you like a two-year credit.
But, at that point, I had already been here a long time.
And in fact, I try to get-- and before that, I tried to become a citizen.
And the reason I was never able to get to the application stage, because I was never able to account for where I was for the last five years.
And you basically had to account for almost every month of where you were.
Well, you ask me in those days, where were you for the last five years?
I would have three pages.
I was just bumming around all over this country.
And, so, I would sit down-- once or twice, I would sit down at the immigration bureau or the federal bureau, and I could not literally complete the application.
So, I would walk out.
You know, like, I was literally like a stateless person.
So, one thing is, with the Navy-- I was in the Navy for four years.
I mean, all I had to do on my application was say, US Navy, four years.
Boom!
They had me.
And, in fact, I did become a citizen about six months before I was discharged.
November of 1961.
I finally decided I was going to become a citizen.
It was a great time.
I even changed my name at that time.
Because I was known as Burlowitz all along, my mother's maiden name.
And so-- And, so, in November of 1961, I was still in the Navy.
I was scheduled to be discharged in May of '62.
And I went ahead in San Diego and put my application in, did my studying, and then I became a citizen of America.
I changed my name.
Right.
They gave you one free name change.
I changed my name to Daniels.
And why did I change my name to Daniels?
I had found out that my real father's name was Daniels only a few years earlier.
At that time, I was corresponding with my mother.
And I told her that I needed to-- in fact, I think prior to that, when I joined the Navy, I had to have proof of birth.
I had no proof of citizenship.
I wasn't a citizen.
So, she was able to send me my birth certificate, and it had my real father's name on it.
It didn't register very much then.
But at the time when I filled out the application, it said, you may change your name if you'd like, because I had recognized that many people had very unpronounceable names.
And I said, hmm.
And I guess there was a psychological hurt that was still there, and, so, I changed my name to my father's name, which certainly didn't-- I'm sure my mother didn't like.

She would liked for me to have changed by name to her then married name which was Curlander, which was a very nice name, which, in retrospect, I wouldn't have mind having Curlander as opposed to Daniels, because Curlander is more of a Jewish name than Daniels.
So, anyway, I changed my name to Daniels at that time, and I became a citizen of America.
It was a wonderful day for me, a wonderful day.
Do you remember it?
Obviously?
Well, I remember it.
The federal building in San Diego.
I remember being with a lot of people.
I remember standing up there and taking the vow, feeling very good, having a few friends there, and going through, at that time, changing some of my records with the Navy.
They had to change a lot of my records.
You know, they had to-- I had to make sure that my discharge papers also were consistent with my military record.
They also had two names on it and all that.
I made sure that that was there, so there wasn't a gap of who was who.
So, yeah, I remember that.
It was a good time.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, then I finished my tour of duty in San Diego.
And I met-- interestingly how anti-Semitism just keeps-- no matter how much sun there is, anti-Semitism keeps coming back into your life.
I had met a girl.
We were beginning to have a relationship, and she was from Latvia.
She was not Jewish.
But she was going at that point-- she was a student at San Diego State.
And I started to-- I was still in the service.
And I had looked up to her, because she was a college student.
A lot of her friends were college students.
At that point, I didn't even have a high-school education.
Although I was always a good communicator, I was able to communicate pretty well with, you know, on a peer level, I still was looking up to her.
Yeah.
Exactly.
Well, I didn't realize.
You know?
To me-- so, but she was very nice, and she influenced me.
Do a little bit with your life.
I was a lifeguard at that time.
You're not going to be a lifeguard all your life.
So, I went to get my-- I went to get a GED.
I went to a local high school in San Diego while I was still in the service.
I attended a few classes.
I was able to get a GED.
They were able to put an actual physical high school on the GED, so it wasn't just by mail order.
So, I left the service with a high-school diploma, and I met her at San Diego State.
We were having a wonderful relationship, but her mother hated me.
Yes.
You know, I was very nice.
You know, for the most part, I'm a likable guy.
You know, I've learned to be nice.
I'm not an in-your-face guy.
I wasn't trying to be arrogant or boring.
I said, why doesn't woman like me?
I always tried to open up the door for her, be a good boy scout.
Well, I found out that she went through the war in Latvia as a non-Jew.
And wouldn't you know it?
The few Jewish people that were living in Latvia at that time in her hometown had money, and they were charging interest while they're needing the money and whatever happened there.
She came to America 20 years later, and every Jew in America was still a dirty pig, because 20 years earlier, some Jew was charging money during the war.
But that was her excuse.
You know, and she knew-- at that point, I didn't talk very much about-- I didn't talk very much about my background.
It wasn't until years later that I started talking about what I went through, because I learned years ago just to shut up.
I didn't want people feeling sorry for me, and, at that point, people didn't didn't know how to take all this.
But they were from Europe.
They knew I was Jewish.
She had confidence in me.
I felt close to her.
She knew I was in a camp.
This woman had not one bit of sympathy for me that I was in a camp.
As far she know, I should have just perished with the rest of them.
